medications.indication_rationale: Oral antihistamine to reduce pruritus and help break the itch-scratch cycle and improve sleep.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally stat then daily (as per source note)

medications.drug_name: gentle non-soap cleanser + topical acne therapy (once skin healed)
medications.indication_rationale: Gentle cleansing now; introduce topical acne therapy (e.g., benzoyl peroxide ± retinoid) once excoriations have healed, via GP plan.
No established superinfection (afebrile, no crusts/spread); reserve for if screening becomes positive.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply hydrocolloid patches over raw nodules as a protective barrier and wear soft cotton gloves at night to prevent picking.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Barrier protection and reduced picking allow rapid healing and reduce infection/scarring risk.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Support staff to provide daily saline wound cleansing and supervise the skin.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Structured caregiver wound care supports healing and early detection of infection.

patient_education_points.point: The deep itch is from the skin flare — the anti-itch medicine and protective patches let the sore spots heal and stop the stinging.
Leave the patches on until they fall off, and use the gloves at night so the skin isn't scratched open again.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Wound healing, signs of infection, and set up a long-term acne plan; involve support staff.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Fever >38°C, spreading redness, skin hot to touch, or unmanageable agitation — seek urgent/emergency review.

must_include_items: Identify excoriated acne vulgaris with an itch-scratch cycle; screen for secondary infection and eczema herpeticum
Break the itch-scratch cycle (antihistamine, barrier patches, cotton gloves) and provide wound care
Involve support staff given the communication barrier
Explicit safety-net for superinfection and unmanageable agitation
errors_of_commission: Labelling as delusional parasitosis / primary psychiatric and missing the treatable acne
Starting systemic antibiotics without evidence of infection
Prescribing sedatives to control agitation as the primary intervention
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires attributing the crisis to primary skin disease, breaking the itch-scratch cycle with barrier protection, involving support staff, and safety-netting for infection.
